questions.text: The hormone which controls the uptake of Sodium ions in kidney and its maintenance in blood plasma is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Growth hormone (GH), also called somatotropin or human growth hormone, is a peptide hormone secreted by the anterior lobe of the pituitary gland. It stimulates the growth of essentially all tissues of the body, including bone.
Aldosterone is a steroid hormone. Its main role is to regulate salt and water in the body, thus having an effect on blood pressure.

Gonadotropins: Luteinizing and Follicle Stimulating Hormones. Luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) are called gonadotropins because they stimulate the gonads - in males, the testes, and in females, the ovaries.
Thyroxine is the main hormone secreted into the bloodstream by the thyroid gland. It plays vital roles in digestion, heart and muscle function, brain development and maintenance of bones.

questions.text: _ hormone is released from the posterior lobe of the pituitary gland.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adrenaline is produced by the adrenal glands and is involved in the body's 'fight or flight' response, not by the pituitary gland.
TSH is produced by the anterior pituitary gland and stimulates the thyroid gland, not the posterior pituitary.
FSH is also released by the anterior pituitary gland and plays a role in reproductive processes.
ADH, also known as vasopressin, is produced in the hypothalamus and stored and released by the posterior pituitary gland. It helps regulate water balance in the body.

questions.explanation: The posterior lobe of the pituitary gland produces two hormones, vasopressin (Antidiuretic hormone) and oxytocin. Adrenaline is released from the adrenal glands and its major action, together with noradrenaline, is to prepare the body for 'fight or flight'. Thyroid-stimulating hormone, also known as TSH, is a glycoprotein hormone produced by the anterior pituitary. Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) is released by the anterior lobe of the pituitary gland.

questions.text: The prolactin hormone, responsible for the activation of mammary glands to start producing milk, is a hormone of the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Prolactin is a peptide hormone secreted by the lactotroph cells in the anterior lobe of the pituitary gland. Therefore, option A is the correct answer.
Option B is incorrect because the pancreas produces insulin and Glucagon, but not prolactin.
Option C is incorrect because the thyroid gland is responsible for producing T3 and T4.
Option D is incorrect because the thymus gland releases several hormones, including thymosin.
Option E is incorrect because the adrenal gland produces a wide variety of hormones such as cortisol, aldosterone androgens, adrenaline, epinephrine, and norepinephrine.

questions.explanation: In humans, prolactin is produced both in the front portion of the pituitary gland (anterior pituitary gland) and in a range of sites elsewhere in the body. Lactotroph cells in the pituitary gland produce prolactin, where it is stored and then released into the bloodstream.

questions.text: The gland known as the" gland of emergency" is the
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While the pituitary gland controls many important bodily functions by producing a variety of hormones, it is not typically referred to as the gland of emergency.

The adrenal glands are often referred to as the "glands of emergency" because they play a crucial role in the body's stress response. These glands produce hormones such as adrenaline and cortisol, which prepare the body to respond to stressful situations by increasing heart rate, raising blood pressure, and mobilizing energy resources.
The thyroid gland plays a significant role in regulating metabolism, but it is not commonly known as the gland of emergency.
The parathyroid glands regulate calcium levels in the body but are not called the gland of emergency.
The pancreas produces insulin and other hormones involved in blood sugar regulation and digestion, but it is not known as the gland of emergency.

questions.text: Which body function is controlled through a positive feedback mechanism?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: An example of positive feedback is uterine contractions during childbirth. The hormone oxytocin, made by the endocrine system, stimulates the contraction of the uterus. This produces the pain sensed by the nervous system. In response, more oxytocin is released, which in turn stimulates the contractions even more. This loop of the 'pain induced from contractions stimulating the release of oxytocin, and oxytocin stimulating more contraction continues with increasing intensity' still the fetus is born. This is a positive feedback loop; hence, Option A is correct.
Thermoregulation (if body temperature changes, mechanisms are induced to restore normal levels). It is a negative feedback mechanism.
Blood sugar regulation (insulin lowers blood glucose when levels are high; glucagon raises blood glucose when levels are low). It is a negative feedback mechanism.

Thyroxine Regulation. The two scenarios involved with thyroxine regulation are stated below. Low thyroxine levels in the bloodstream stimulate the hypothalamus to release TRH (Thyrotropin-releasing hormone), and this causes the pituitary to release TSH (Thyroid-stimulating hormone) so the thyroid releases more thyroxine, and the blood levels return to normal. 2) Normal thyroxine levels in the bloodstream inhibit TRH release from the hypothalamus, and this inhibits the release of TSH from the pituitary, so less thyroxine is released from the thyroid gland and normal blood levels are maintained. It is a negative feedback mechanism.

questions.explanation: A feedback mechanism is a loop system wherein the system responds to a perturbation. The response may be in the same direction (as in positive feedback) or in the opposite direction (as in negative feedback). An example of positive feedback is uterine contractions during childbirth. The hormone oxytocin, made by the endocrine system, stimulates the contraction of the uterus. This produces the pain sensed by the nervous system. In response, more oxytocin is released, which in turn stimulates the contractions even more. This loop of the 'pain induced from contractions stimulating the release of oxytocin, and oxytocin stimulating more contraction continues with increasing intensity' still the fetus is born. This is a positive feedback loop; hence, Option A is correct.

questions.text: Which of the following is not secreted by thyroid gland?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Thyroxin, also known as T4, is a hormone secreted by the thyroid gland. It plays a crucial role in regulating metabolism and energy production in the body.
Triiodothyronine, or T3, is another hormone produced by the thyroid gland. It is essential for various physiological processes, including metabolism, growth, and development.
Calcitonin is a hormone produced by the C-cells of the thyroid gland. It helps regulate calcium levels in the blood by lowering them when necessary.
Glucagon is a hormone that is not secreted by the thyroid gland. Instead, it is produced by the alpha cells of the pancreas and plays a key role in increasing blood glucose levels.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Glucagon. Glucagon is secreted by the pancreas, not the thyroid gland. Its primary function is to increase blood glucose levels, which is crucial for maintaining energy balance in the body. On the other hand, Thyroxin (T4), Triiodothyronine (T3), and Calcitonin are all hormones secreted by the thyroid gland. Thyroxin and Triiodothyronine are involved in regulating metabolism, while Calcitonin helps control calcium levels in the blood.

questions.text: Which hormone is chemically a steroid?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: ADH or Vasopressin is essentially a polypeptide hormone.
Thyroxine is essentially an amino acid derivative.
Cortisone is essentially a steroid hormone.
Insulin is essentially a protein hormone.

questions.explanation: Steroid hormones are fat-soluble molecules made from cholesterol. Among these are the three major sex hormone groups: estrogens, androgens, and progesterones. Males and females make all three, just in different amounts. The steroids that are made almost exclusively in the adrenal glands are cortisol, 11-deoxycortisol, aldosterone, corticosterone, and 11-deoxycorticosterone. Most other steroid hormones, including estrogens, are made by the adrenal glands and the gonads.

questions.text: Due to the deficiency of _ hormone, synthesis of glycogen gets depressed, glucose level increases in blood and leads to a condition called Diabetes mellitus.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Oxytocin is a hormone produced by the hypothalamus and released by the posterior pituitary gland. It plays a role in various reproductive functions, such as stimulating uterine contractions during childbirth and promoting milk ejection during breastfeeding. Oxytocin is not directly involved in the regulation of blood glucose levels or the synthesis of glycogen.

Parathormone, also known as parathyroid hormone (PTH), is produced by the parathyroid glands and plays a critical role in calcium and phosphate homeostasis. It regulates the levels of these minerals in the blood and bones. Parathormone does not have a direct role in the synthesis of glycogen or the regulation of blood glucose levels.

Glucagon is a hormone produced by the alpha cells of the pancreas. It increases blood glucose levels by stimulating the breakdown of glycogen (glycogenolysis) in the liver and promoting the synthesis of glucose (gluconeogenesis). In cases of low blood glucose, glucagon is released to help raise blood sugar levels. It is the hormone that acts opposite to insulin.

Diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disorder characterized by high levels of glucose in the blood due to the inability of the body to produce or respond to the hormone insulin. Insulin is produced by the beta cells of the pancreas and is responsible for glucose uptake from the blood into the cells, where it can be used as energy or stored as glycogen. When there is a deficiency of insulin, as in the case of diabetes, glycogen synthesis gets depressed, and glucose levels increase in the blood.

Cortisol is a hormone produced by the adrenal glands in response to stress. It plays a role in regulating various metabolic processes, including glucose metabolism. Cortisol can increase blood glucose levels by promoting gluconeogenesis (the synthesis of glucose from non-carbohydrate sources) and reducing cell glucose uptake. However, it is not primarily associated with the synthesis of glycogen or the development of diabetes mellitus.

questions.explanation: Diabetes mellitus is a metabolic disorder characterized by high levels of glucose in the blood due to the inability of the body to produce or respond to the hormone insulin. Insulin is produced by the beta cells of the pancreas and is responsible for glucose uptake from the blood into the cells, where it can be used as energy or stored as glycogen. When insulin is deficient, as in the case of diabetes, glycogen synthesis gets depressed, and glucose levels increase in the blood.

questions.text: One function of the parasympathetic nervous system is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The contraction of hair muscles, also known as arrector pili muscles, is primarily regulated by the sympathetic nervous system. It causes hair to stand on end, a response known as piloerection.

Sweat gland activity is mainly regulated by the sympathetic nervous system. The sympathetic system promotes the production and release of sweat in response to heat or stress, not the parasympathetic system.
The acceleration of the heart rate is controlled by the sympathetic nervous system. It triggers the "fight or flight" response, increasing heart rate to prepare the body for action.

This is the correct answer. The parasympathetic nervous system is responsible for constricting the pupil (making it smaller) in response to non-stressful, restful situations. This action allows for better near vision and reduces the amount of light entering the eye.

questions.explanation: The action of the parasympathetic nervous system is opposite to that of the sympathetic nervous system. If the sympathetic nervous system accelerates an action, the parasympathetic nervous system slows it. However, neither system is exclusively excitatory or inhibitory. The parasympathetic fibres constrict the pupil, decrease the rate and force of heartbeat, dilate many blood vessels, lower the arterial blood pressure, quicken the peristaltic movements, and contract the urinary bladder.

questions.text: Injury localized to the hypothalamus would most likely disrupt:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Short-term memory is mainly associated with the hippocampus in the temporal lobe, not the hypothalamus.

Coordination during locomotion is controlled mainly by the cerebellum, not the hypothalamus. The cerebellum integrates sensory input with motor commands to maintain balance, posture, and smooth, coordinated movements while walking or running.

options.text: Executive functions; such as decision making
options.explanation: Executive functions such as decision-making, planning, and judgment are primarily controlled by the frontal lobe of the cerebrum, especially the prefrontal cortex. The hypothalamus, on the other hand, mainly regulates autonomic functions like hunger, thirst, temperature, and hormone release, not higher executive tasks.

The hypothalamus lies at the base of the thalamus. It provides an anatomical connection between the nervous and endocrine systems through its relationship to the pituitary gland. The hypothalamus is a thermoregulatory centre. Hence, it is called the “thermostat” of the body. It keeps body temperature at roughly 37°C by means of a complex thermostat system. Any localised injury to the hypothalamus will, hence, disrupt the regulation of body temperature.

questions.text: A person entering an empty room suddenly finds a snake right in front of him.Which one of the following is likely to happen in his neuro­hormonal control system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The sympathetic nervous system is activated releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal medulla
options.explanation: The correct answer is: The sympathetic nervous system is activated, releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal medulla—this triggers the “fight-or-flight” response for immediate action.

options.text: Neurotransmitters diffuse rapidly across the cleft and transmit a nerve impulse.
options.explanation: Neurotransmitters diffuse rapidly… – True in general nerve transmission, but not the main event in this sudden danger scenario.

options.text: Hypothalamus activates the parasympathetic division of brain
options.explanation: Hypothalamus activates the parasympathetic division… – Parasympathetic is for “rest-and-digest,” not emergency response.

options.text: Sympathetic nervous system is activated releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal cortex.
options.explanation: Sympathetic nervous system… from the adrenal cortex – Adrenal cortex releases steroid hormones (like cortisol, aldosterone), not epinephrine/norepinephrine.

questions.explanation: Hormones epinephrine and norepinephrine are secreted from the adrenal medulla. They are emergency hormones released in conditions of stress, emergency, etc. Epinephrine and norepinephrine are also released by adrenergic nerve fibres of the sympathetic nervous system, where they act as neurotransmitters.

questions.text: Norepinephrine (i)is released by sympathetic fibers (ii)is released by parasympathetic fibers (iii)increases the heart rate (iv)decreases blood pressureWhich of the above statements are correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sympathetic fibers are also called small-fiber nerves. It is developed from the collections of nerve clusters called ganglia. It is available on the outside of the spinal cord. It will increase the rate of heartbeat, muscle contraction, and rate of conduction. So, i is correct. And iii is also correct.

The parasympathetic nervous system consists of cranial nerves, the vagus nerve, and the lumbar spinal nerves. During this nerve stimulation, it increases digestive secretions and also reduces heart rate. The norepinephrine hormone will increase the heartbeat. So, ii is not correct and iii is correct.
Sympathetic fibers release the norepinephrine. Blood pressure is increased during the large amount of norepinephrine hormone excretion. So, i is correct but iv is not the correct answer.
Parasympathetic fibers do not produce the norepinephrine hormone. Decreasing blood pressure is not possible during the excretion of hormones. So, both ii and iv are not correct.

questions.explanation: Norepinephrine is secreted by some neurons of the sympathetic nervous system and also by the adrenal medulla. It accelerates the heart rate. Norepinephrine is a hormone and neurotransmitter that primarily functions to mediate the body's "fight-or-flight" response to stress by increasing heart rate, blood pressure, and blood flow to muscles and the brain. It also plays a crucial role in the brain by regulating attention, focus, alertness, and the sleep-wake cycle. Additionally, it affects mood and memory and is involved in energy regulation through the breakdown of stored fats and glycogen for glucose.

questions.text: Chemically hormones are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, hormones are not chemically carbohydrates. Carbohydrates are organic molecules made of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. They are the body's main source of energy.

Protein hormones are made up of amino acids and are the most common type of hormone. These hormones are produced by the endocrine glands, such as the thyroid gland, the pituitary gland, and the adrenal glands.
Steroid hormones are made up of cholesterol and are produced by the adrenal glands and the gonads (ovaries and testes).

Both B and C are correct because protein hormones are made up of amino acids and are the most common type of hormone. These hormones are produced by the endocrine glands, such as the thyroid gland, the pituitary gland, and the adrenal glands. Steroid hormones are made up of cholesterol and are produced by the adrenal glands and the gonads (ovaries and testes).

These hormones are produced by the endocrine glands, such as the thyroid gland, the pituitary gland, and the adrenal glands. Steroid hormones: Steroid hormones are made up of cholesterol and are produced by the adrenal glands and the gonads (ovaries and testes).

questions.text: How do parasympathetic neural signals affect the working of the heart?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Reduce both heart rate and cardiac output
options.explanation: Parasympathetic neural signals, primarily via the vagus nerve, slow down the heart rate by decreasing the activity of the sinoatrial (SA) node. A lower heart rate results in reduced cardiac output, as it is the product of heart rate and stroke volume. Thus, both heart rate and cardiac output are reduced.

options.text: Heart rate is increased without affecting the cardiac output
options.explanation: This is incorrect as parasympathetic stimulation usually decreases the heart rate. An increase in heart rate would typically increase cardiac output, which is contrary to the parasympathetic effect.

options.text: Both heart rate and cardiac output increase
options.explanation: This choice is incorrect because parasympathetic signals have a calming effect on the heart, leading to a decrease in both heart rate and cardiac output, not an increase.

options.text: Heart rate decreases but cardiac output increases
options.explanation: This is incorrect because a decrease in heart rate typically leads to a decrease in cardiac output. Parasympathetic stimulation results in reduced heart rate and cardiac output.

questions.explanation: Parasympathetic neural signals, mediated by the vagus nerve (cranial nerve X), exert an inhibitory influence on the heart. During parasympathetic stimulation, the heart rate decreases (bradycardia) as the sinoatrial (SA) node's firing rate is reduced. This leads to fewer contractions per minute and consequently lowers cardiac output, which is determined by heart rate and stroke volume. Therefore, the correct answer is that both heart rate and cardiac output are reduced. The other options are incorrect as they do not accurately describe the effects of parasympathetic stimulation on the heart.

questions.text: Which one gives the most valid and recent explanation for stomatal opening?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While starch hydrolysis provides energy for cellular processes, it is not the primary mechanism for stomatal opening according to recent findings.
Photosynthesis in guard cells does contribute to the process, but it is not the most recent or valid explanation for stomatal opening.
Transpiration involves water movement through plants and affects stomatal function, but does not directly explain the mechanism of opening.
The movement of potassium ions into and out of guard cells is the primary mechanism for regulating stomatal aperture, affecting turgor pressure and causing stomata to open or close.

questions.explanation: Modern research shows stomatal opening is driven by ion movements in guard cells. Potassium ions enter guard cells, lowering water potential and causing water uptake. This increases turgor in guard cells, opening the stomata.

questions.text: Which of the following criteria does not pertain to facilitated transport?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Transport saturation occurs in facilitated transport when all available transport proteins are occupied, causing the rate of transport to plateau.

Uphill transport involves moving molecules against their concentration gradient, requiring energy input. Facilitated transport, however, moves substances down their concentration gradient and does not require energy, which is characteristic of passive transport.
Facilitated transport requires specific membrane proteins like carriers or channels to assist in the movement of molecules, which differentiates it from simple diffusion.
Facilitated transport is highly selective, allowing specific molecules or ions to pass through the membrane via specialized proteins, ensuring controlled transport.

questions.explanation: Facilitated transport is a passive process that moves molecules down their concentration gradient with the help of specific membrane proteins. Option B, 'Uphill transport,' is incorrect for facilitated transport because it involves moving substances against their gradient, requiring energy input, which is characteristic of active transport. Option A, 'Transport saturation,' is a valid feature of facilitated transport as it reaches a maximum rate when all proteins are occupied. Option C, 'Requirement of special membrane proteins,' is true since facilitated transport depends on specific proteins to move molecules. Option D, 'High selectivity,' accurately describes facilitated transport as it allows only specific molecules to pass through the membrane.

questions.text: Guard cells help in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Guard cells control the stomatal opening and closing, which directly influences transpiration, the process by which water is lost from the plant through evaporation.
Guttation involves the exudation of water droplets from the edges of leaves, primarily driven by root pressure and not directly controlled by guard cells.
While plants have defense mechanisms against pathogens, guard cells are specifically involved in regulating gas exchange and water loss, not directly combating infections.
Guard cells do not protect plants from herbivores. This role is often fulfilled by physical barriers like thorns or chemical deterrents.

questions.explanation: Stomata are the main organs for transpiration. The stem and leaf epidermis are provided with numerous stomata. Diffusion of water vapour through the stomatal pores is known as stomatal transpiration. Transpiration occurs while the stomata are open for the passage of carbon dioxide and oxygen during photosynthesis. Stomatal opening and closing are regulated by the movement of guard cells. It is a loss of water in droplets form from leaves, but this process occurs under certain conditions. It is not a regular process. Guard cells are photosynthetic cells and help in transpiration. They do not protect against grazing. So the correct option is A.

questions.text: The movement of ions against the concentration gradient will be
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Active transport is the process by which molecules move across a membrane from a lower to a higher concentration, against the gradient. This requires energy, usually in the form of ATP.
Osmosis is the movement of water molecules across a semipermeable membrane from a region of lower solute concentration to a region of higher solute concentration. It doesn't involve ions or energy.
Diffusion is the movement of particles from an area of higher concentration to an area of lower concentration, following the concentration gradient. It does not require energy.
This option is incorrect as active transport is the only process that involves the movement of ions against the concentration gradient and requires energy.

questions.explanation: Active transport is the correct answer because it involves moving ions against the concentration gradient, which requires energy in the form of ATP. Osmosis and diffusion do not require energy and involve movement along the concentration gradient, not against it. 'All of the above' is incorrect because only active transport matches the criteria of moving ions against the gradient with energy consumption.

questions.text: Name the blood cells whose reduction in number causes clotting disorder:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Erythrocytes, commonly known as red blood cells (RBCs), are the most abundant type of blood cells in the human body. Their primary function is to transport oxygen from the lungs to all the tissues in the body and carry carbon dioxide, a waste product of cellular metabolism, back to the lungs for exhalation.
Leukocytes, also known as white blood cells (WBCs), are a diverse group of blood cells that play a crucial role in the immune system. Unlike RBCs, leukocytes have a nucleus and other organelles.
Neutrophils are a type of leukocyte and a specific subset of granulocytes. They are the most abundant type of white blood cells and are essential components of the innate immune system.
Thrombocytes are pieces of very large cells in the bone marrow called megakaryocytes. They help form blood clots to slow or stop bleeding and to help wounds heal.

questions.explanation: Thrombocytes (platelets) initiate clot formation. A reduced count causes bleeding disorders.

questions.text: Serum differs from blood in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Globulins are proteins in the blood, including antibodies, and are present in both serum and plasma. Serum contains globulins, so this option is incorrect.
Albumins are vital proteins for maintaining osmotic pressure and are present in both serum and plasma. Serum is not lacking albumins, making this option incorrect.
Serum is derived from blood plasma and lacks clotting factors, such as fibrinogen, which are used during coagulation. Therefore, this option is correct.
Serum contains antibodies, which are important immune proteins and a type of globulin. Antibodies are present in serum, so this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Serum is plasma without clotting proteins like fibrinogen, which are consumed during coagulation.

questions.text: Which one of the following is correct?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Lymph is a clear fluid that is part of the lymphatic system and primarily contains lymphocytes (a type of white blood cell), but it does not contain red blood cells (RBCs) or significant amounts of plasma proteins.

This option is correct. Blood is composed of plasma, the liquid portion, and formed elements, which include red blood cells (RBCs), white blood cells (WBCs), and platelets, making it a complete transport medium in the body.

This option is incorrect. Plasma is the liquid component of blood that remains after the removal of all cellular elements, not just lymphocytes. It contains water, salts, enzymes, antibodies, and other proteins.
This option is incorrect. Serum is derived from plasma but lacks clotting factors like fibrinogen because it is the fluid that remains after blood has clotted.

questions.explanation: Blood is a fluid connective tissue that circulates through our body and performs various functions, including transport of oxygen, nutrients, hormones, and waste products. It consists of plasma, which is the liquid portion of blood, and formed elements, including red blood cells (RBCs), white blood cells (WBCs), and platelets.

questions.text: The diagram given here is the standard ECG of a normal person. The P-­wave represents the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The systole refers to the phase of the cardiac cycle when the heart muscle contracts. The P-wave on an electrocardiogram (ECG) represents the electrical depolarization and subsequent contraction of the atria, not the beginning of systole. The P-wave indicates the initiation of the electrical activity that leads to the contraction of the atria, rather than the beginning of systole itself.

The end of systole is not represented by the P-wave on an ECG. Systole encompasses both atrial contraction and ventricular contraction, and the P-wave specifically represents atrial depolarization and contraction, rather than the end of systole.

This option is correct. The P-wave on an ECG represents the depolarization and subsequent contraction of the atria. It signifies the electrical activity that initiates the contraction of the atria, pumping blood from the atria to the ventricles.

The initiation of ventricular contraction is not represented by the P-wave on an ECG. The ventricular contraction is initiated by the electrical activity that follows the P-wave, specifically the QRS complex. The QRS complex represents the depolarization and subsequent contraction of the ventricles, leading to the ejection of blood from the heart.

questions.explanation: P-wave represents the electrical excitation (or depolarisation) of the atria, which leads to the contraction of both the atria. The QRS complex represents the depolarisation of the ventricles, which initiates the ventricular contraction. The contraction starts shortly after Q and marks the beginning of the systole. The T wave represents the return of the ventricles from excited to normal state. (repolarisation). The end of the T-wave marks the end of systole.

questions.text: Which one of the following human organs is often called the “graveyard” of RBCs?

options.explanation: The gall bladder is not known as the "graveyard" of RBCs. It stores bile produced by the liver and releases it into the small intestine to aid in digestion.
The kidney filters waste products and excess substances from the blood to form urine. It is not commonly referred to as the "graveyard" of RBCs.

The human organ that is often called the "graveyard" of red blood cells (RBCs) is the spleen. The spleen plays a role in removing old, damaged, or worn-out red blood cells from circulation. It also helps recycle components of RBCs, such as iron, for reuse in the body.

The liver is involved in various metabolic processes, including the synthesis and breakdown of molecules. While the liver can be involved in the breakdown of old blood cells, it is not commonly referred to as the "graveyard" of RBCs.

options.text: Break up into capillaries, then form veins

Visceral arteries branch off of the abdominal aorta and supply blood to the stomach, intestines, liver, spleen, pancreas, and kidneys.

questions.text: If due to some injury, the chordae tendinae of the tricuspid valve of the human heart is partially non-­functional. What will be the immediate effect?

options.text: The flow of blood into the aorta will be slowed down
options.explanation: This option is not directly related to the tricuspid valve or the chordae tendinae. The aorta is a major artery that carries oxygenated blood from the left ventricle of the heart to the rest of the body. Issues with the tricuspid valve would not directly impact the flow of blood into the aorta.

The 'pacemaker' refers to the sinoatrial (SA) node, which is responsible for initiating the electrical signals that regulate the heart's rhythm. Damage to the chordae tendinae of the tricuspid valve would not directly affect the function of the SA node, which is located in the right atrium.

options.text: The blood will tend to flow back into the left atrium
options.explanation: This option is not accurate. The tricuspid valve is located between the right atrium and right ventricle, so damage to its chordae tendinae would affect the flow of blood between these two chambers, not into the left atrium.

options.text: The flow of blood into the pulmonary artery will be reduced
options.explanation: Correct. The tricuspid valve is located on the right side of the heart and prevents backflow of blood from the right ventricle into the right atrium. If the chordae tendinae are partially non-functional, the tricuspid valve may not close properly, leading to regurgitation of blood from the right ventricle into the right atrium. This can result in a reduction of blood flow into the pulmonary artery, which carries blood from the right ventricle to the lungs.

However, lymphocytes are important for the immune system because they produce antibodies, which help the body to fight off infection.

Neutrophils and monocytes are the most active phagocytic white blood cells in the human body. Phagocytosis is the process of engulfing and destroying foreign particles, such as bacteria and viruses. They are the first line of defense against infection, and they are quickly recruited to the site of infection. They circulate in the blood for a few days before migrating to tissues, where they become macrophages. Macrophages are long-lived cells that are able to phagocytose large amounts of material. When resistances are connected in parallel, the equivalent resistance is equal to the sum of the reciprocal of individual resistances. As the voltage across each resistor remains constant and the current splits across each resistor according to Kirchoff’s junction rule. Hence the equivalent resistance in this case is the sum of the reciprocal of individual resistances. I = I1+I2 =V1/R1 + V2/R2 = V/R1 + V/R2 = V ( 1/R1+ 1/R2) = V/Req =1/Req = (1/R1+ 1/R2)

questions.text: The only o, p−directing group which is deactivating in nature is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The amino group (-NH2) is an ortho, para-directing group, meaning it directs incoming groups to the ortho and para positions of the benzene ring. However, it is a strongly activating group, not deactivating. It activates the ring through resonance and electron donation, making it a strong activator of electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions.

The hydroxyl group (-OH) is also an ortho, para-directing group. Similar to the amino group, it activates the benzene ring through resonance and electron donation. It is not deactivating in nature and therefore does not match the criteria for the question.

Halogens are ortho and para directors, but deactivators. Overall, they remove electron density from the ring as they are more electronegative than carbon, making them less reactive. However, due to their resonance donation to the ring ( which is because each halogen has a lone pair of electrons), if it does react, it reacts primarily at the ortho and para positions. Through resonance, a halogen tends to stabilize the carbocation formed during electrophilic substitution, and the effect is more pronounced at ortho- and para-positions. However, the inductive effect is stronger than resonance here and causes net electron withdrawal and thus net deactivation. The resonance effect tends to oppose the inductive effect for the attack at ortho- and para-positions and hence makes the deactivation less for ortho- and para-attack.

Alkyl groups (-R) are weakly activating groups, not deactivating. They donate electron density to the benzene ring through inductive effects, making the ring slightly more electron-rich. Alkyl groups do not have a strong directing effect and generally do not influence the position of incoming groups during electrophilic aromatic substitution reactions.

questions.explanation: Halides are ortho-para- directing groups but unlike most ortho-para- directors halides tend to deactivate benzene. This unusual behaviour can be explained by two properties:1. Since the halogens are very electronegative they cause inductive withdrawal (withdrawal of electrons from the carbon atom of benzene).2. Since the halogens have non-bonding electrons they can donate electron density through pi bonding (resonance donation).Option C is correct.

questions.text: Order of reactivity of halogens towards alkanes is:

options.explanation: Alkanes undergo free radical substitution reactions with halogens, as they are sp3 hybridised and halogens can only be added in the molecule by replacing another group (substituting). The mechanism of free radical substitution involves 3 steps: initiation, propagation and termination. In the first step, the diatomic halogen molecules are split into two free radical species which can then add to the alkane molecules in the propagation steps. Down the group, the electronegativity decreases while the stability of these free radicals increases. The fluorine free radical is the least stable and most electronegative so it attacks the alkane more readily, and releases the highest amount of energy in the propagation step. The next is chlorine, followed by bromine and finally iodine, which is the least reactive. In Fact it is so unreactive that it is unable to extract a H atom from alkane. Fluorine is the most reactive halogen and is highly electronegative, leading to the formation of the strongest carbon-fluorine bond. Chlorine is less reactive than fluorine but still more reactive than bromine and iodine. Bromine is less reactive than both fluorine and chlorine but more reactive than iodine. questions.text: Nitrogen and phosphorus have 3 of their valence electrons unpaired because of:

questions.text: The unit of rate constant is same as that of rate of reaction in:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: In a first-order reaction, the rate law is expressed as Rate = k[A]. The unit of the rate constant (k) is time-1, while the unit of the rate of reaction is concentration unit per time, such as mol dm-3 s-1. Therefore, the units are not the same.

In a zero-order reaction, the rate law is Rate = k. The rate constant (k) has the same unit as the rate of reaction, which is usually concentration unit per time, such as mol dm-3 s-1. Thus, the units match.

For a second-order reaction, the rate law might be Rate = k[A]2. The unit of the rate constant (k) is typically (concentration unit)-1 * time-1, whereas the rate of reaction is concentration unit per time. Therefore, the units do not match.

In a third-order reaction, the rate law can be expressed as Rate = k[A]3. The unit of the rate constant (k) is (concentration unit)-2 * time-1. The rate of reaction has a unit of concentration unit per time. Clearly, the units are not the same.

questions.explanation: Rate Law: Rate = k The unit of the rate constant (k) is typically expressed as concentration unit per time. options.explanation: “Too” is an adverb of degree that can mean “excessively,” “also,” or “very.” It is used before an adjective or adverb or before “much”, “many”, “few” etc. It is used when the speaker wants to show so much of a particular quality that something is not possible (Example: I was too excited to sleep.) or when the speaker wants to express that something is more than necessary or acceptable (Example: You’re driving too fast.). It is also used after mentioning an additional person, thing, or fact to show that they are also included in what you are saying (Example: Sana’s got a lovely voice and she’s a good dancer too.).
“To” is a preposition. It can be used to indicate a goal or a direction of movement, as well as a place of arrival. Thus, choosing “to” to complete the given sentence would be most appropriate.

“Of” and “to” are two separate prepositions that can’t be used as a prepositional phrase because it only possible to use two prepositions together if the second one is creating a noun phrase. Hence, “of to” does not make sense grammatically.

The verb “get” is an irregular verb. Its three parts are get, got, gotten. It is used as get + object or get + indirect object + direct object when the speaker wants to express the meaning of “fetch” or “receive” or “obtain” (Example: Can you get me a glass of water?). It is also used as a get + adjective to describe a change of state or situation (Example: Let’s hope she gets better soon.).

questions.text: Demonstrate correct use of articles and prepositions to clarify meaning:He started learning English _ 2005.
questions.subject: English

options.explanation: We use “in” before years because a year is a general period of time, not a specific day or moment. Prepositions like “on” and “at” are used only for exact dates or precise times, but “2005” is neither. Therefore, the correct preposition for mentioning the year someone began learning English is “in,” making the sentence “He started learning English in 2005” grammatically correct.

We use “on” only for specific days and dates, like “on Monday” or “on 5th March.” The year 2005 is not a specific day—it is a whole 12-month period. Since “on” cannot be used for years, it would be grammatically wrong to say “on 2005.” That’s why the correct preposition is “in 2005.”

We use “at” for exact, pinpoint times—like at 5 PM, at midnight, or at sunrise. The year 2005 is not an exact moment but a long period, so “at” cannot be used with it. Since “at” doesn’t work for years, saying “at 2005” is grammatically incorrect. The only correct preposition for years is “in 2005.”

The word “through” means from one point to the end of another, covering the whole period (e.g., through the night, through the month). But in the sentence “He started learning English _ 2005”, we are not talking about a period he continued through—only the starting point. Since “through” indicates duration, not a starting year, “through 2005” doesn’t fit the meaning, so it is incorrect.

questions.explanation: We use “in” before years because a year is a general period of time, not a specific day or moment. Prepositions like “on” and “at” are used only for exact dates or precise times, but “2005” is neither. Therefore, the correct preposition for mentioning the year someone began learning English is “in,” making the sentence “He started learning English in 2005” grammatically correct.

questions.text: Statement:The Management of School M has decided to give free breakfast from next academic year to all the students in its primary section through its canteen even though they will not get any government grant.Courses of Action:I. The school will have to admit many poor students who will seek admission for the next academic year.II. The canteen facilities and utensils have to be checked and new purchases to be made to equip it properly.III. Funds will have to be raised to support the scheme for years to come.
questions.subject: Logical Reasoning

options.explanation: Let's analyze each course of action in response to the given statement:Statement: The Management of School M has decided to give free breakfast from next academic year to all the students in its primary section through its canteen even though they will not get any government grant.Courses of Action:I. The school will have to admit many poor students who will seek admission for the next academic year.II. The canteen facilities and utensils have to be checked, and new purchases to be made to equip it properly.III. Funds will have to be raised to support the scheme for years to come.Let's evaluate each course of action:I. The school will have to admit many poor students who will seek admission for the next academic year.This course of action suggests that offering free breakfast to all students in the primary section may attract many poor students to seek admission at the school. Providing free breakfast could be a positive incentive for economically disadvantaged families to consider enrolling their children in the school.II. The canteen facilities and utensils have to be checked, and new purchases to be made to equip it properly.This course of action suggests that the canteen facilities and utensils need to be assessed and upgraded to ensure they can cater to the increased demand for providing free breakfast to all primary students. Proper equipment and facilities are essential for efficiently running the breakfast program.III. Funds will have to be raised to support the scheme for years to come.This course of action highlights the need for raising funds to sustain the free breakfast program in the long term. Offering free breakfast to all primary students will involve ongoing costs for procuring food items and maintaining the canteen, especially if the school is not receiving any government grant for the initiative.To summarize, the courses of action outline some steps that the school management needs to consider after deciding to provide free breakfast to all primary students. These steps include potentially admitting more students, checking and upgrading the canteen facilities, and raising funds to sustain the program. The success of the initiative will depend on effective planning and resource management by the school management.
